--     |&gt; Application.withEventStore postgresConfig
--     |&gt; Application.withService myService
--   
--   main = Application.run app
--   </pre>
--   
--   After <a>run</a>, the config is accessible via:
--   
--   <ul>
--   <li>Implicit parameter: <tt>?config</tt> (with <tt>HasAppConfig</tt>
--   constraint)</li>
--   <li>Explicit accessor: <tt>Config.get @AppConfig</tt></li>
--   </ul>
withConfig :: (HasParser config, Typeable config) => Application -> Application

-- | Configure the EventStore for the Application.
--   
--   The EventStore factory takes a function from your config type to the
--   EventStore configuration. If you don't need app config, use
--   <tt>()</tt> as the config type and ignore the parameter:
--   
--   <pre>
--   -- Static config (no app config needed):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withEventStore @() (\_ -&gt; postgresConfig)
--   
--   -- Config-dependent (uses app config):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withConfig @AppConfig
--     |&gt; Application.withEventStore (\cfg -&gt; makePostgresConfig cfg)
--   </pre>
--   
--   When the config type is <tt>()</tt>, the factory is evaluated
--   immediately and no <a>withConfig</a> is required. Otherwise,
--   <a>withConfig</a> must be called first and the factory is evaluated
--   during <a>run</a>.
withEventStore :: (Typeable config, EventStoreConfig eventStoreConfig) => (config -> eventStoreConfig) -> Application -> Application

-- | Configure the QueryObjectStore for the Application.
--   
--   The QueryObjectStore is used to store query read models. If not
--   configured, queries will use in-memory storage by default.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   let postgresConfig = PostgresQueryObjectStore
--         { connectionString = "..." }
--   
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withQueryObjectStore postgresConfig
--     |&gt; Application.withQuery @CartSummary
--   </pre>
withQueryObjectStore :: QueryObjectStoreConfig config => config -> Application -> Application

-- | Register a query type with automatic wiring.
--   
--   This is the declarative way to add queries to an Application. At
--   runtime, when <a>runWith</a> is called, the query infrastructure is
--   automatically created:
--   
--   <ul>
--   <li>QueryObjectStore for storing query instances (using the configured
--   backend)</li>
--   <li>EntityFetcher for reconstructing entity state (for each entity in
--   EntitiesOf query)</li>
--   <li>QueryUpdater for handling entity events (for each entity)</li>
--   <li>HTTP endpoint at <tt>GET <i>queries</i>{query-name}</tt></li>
--   </ul>
--   
--   The query name is derived from 'NameOf query' in kebab-case. All
--   entities listed in 'EntitiesOf query' are automatically wired.
--   
--   The QueryObjectStore backend is determined by
--   <a>withQueryObjectStore</a>. If not configured, in-memory storage is
--   used by default.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withTransport WebTransport.server
--     |&gt; Application.withService cartService
--     |&gt; Application.withQuery @CartSummary
--   </pre>
withQuery :: forall query (queryName :: Symbol) (entities :: [Type]). (Query query, ToSchema query, ToJSON query, FromJSON query, queryName ~ NameOf query, entities ~ EntitiesOf query, KnownSymbol queryName, WireEntities entities query) => Application -> Application

-- | Add a query endpoint to the Application.
--   
--   Query endpoints expose read models via HTTP GET
--   <i>queries</i>{query-name}. The handler is a Task that returns JSON
--   text when called.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   cartSummaryStore &lt;- InMemory.new
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withQueryEndpoint "cart-summary" (Endpoint.createQueryEndpoint cartSummaryStore)
--   </pre>
withQueryEndpoint :: Text -> QueryEndpointHandler -> Application -> Application

-- | Add a ServiceRunner to the Application.
--   
--   ServiceRunners are functions that run services with a shared
--   EventStore. Multiple runners can be added and they will all be
--   executed when the Application is run.
withServiceRunner :: ServiceRunner -> Application -> Application

-- | Add a Service to the Application.
--   
--   This converts the Service to a ServiceRunner and adds it to the
--   Application. The Service will use the Application's shared EventStore
--   instead of creating its own.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withService myCartService
--     |&gt; Application.withService myOrderService
--   </pre>
withService :: forall (cmds :: Row Type) (commandTransportNames :: [Symbol]) event entity. (event ~ ServiceEventType cmds, entity ~ ServiceEntityType cmds, FromJSON event, ToJSON event, FromJSON entity, ToJSON entity, KnownSymbol (NameOf entity)) => Service cmds commandTransportNames -> Application -> Application

-- | Add a Transport to the Application.
--   
--   Transports define how commands are exposed to external clients (e.g.,
--   HTTP, gRPC). Multiple transports can be configured, and they will all
--   be started when the Application runs.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withTransport WebTransport.server
--   </pre>
withTransport :: forall transport (transportName :: Symbol). (Transport transport, transportName ~ NameOf transport, KnownHash transportName, KnownSymbol transportName) => transport -> Application -> Application

-- | Register a typed outbound integration handler.
--   
--   Each handler is a type that implements OutboundIntegration (via the TH
--   macro). The handler's entity type, event type, and processing logic
--   are all encoded in the type — no function parameters needed.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withOutbound @ReserveStockOnItemAdded
--     |&gt; Application.withOutbound @NotifyOwnerOnCartCreated
--   </pre>
withOutbound :: (OutboundIntegration handler, entity ~ EntityOf handler, event ~ EventOf entity, event ~ HandledEvent handler, FromJSON entity, FromJSON event, FromJSON (EventOf entity), Inspectable entity, Default entity, Entity entity) => Application -> Application

-- | Register an outbound integration with lifecycle management.
--   
--   Use this for integrations that need expensive resources like:
--   
--   <ul>
--   <li>Database connection pools</li>
--   <li>Embedded interpreters (Lua, Python, etc.)</li>
--   <li>gRPC channels</li>
--   <li>WebSocket subscriptions</li>
--   </ul>
--   
--   Workers are created per-entity. When a worker is idle for too long, it
--   is reaped and cleanup is called. The next event for that entity will
--   spawn a fresh worker with initialize called again.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   -- Static (no config needed):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withService cartService
--     |&gt; Application.withOutboundLifecycle @() @CartEntity (_ -&gt; postgresNotifier)
--   
--   -- Config-dependent:
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withConfig @MyConfig
--     |&gt; Application.withOutboundLifecycle @MyConfig @CartEntity (cfg -&gt; makeNotifier cfg)
--   </pre>
withOutboundLifecycle :: (Typeable config, Inspectable entity) => (config -> OutboundConfig state) -> Application -> Application

-- | Register an inbound integration worker.
--   
--   Inbound integrations listen to external sources (timers, webhooks,
--   message queues) and translate external events into domain commands.
--   
--   When the Application runs, each inbound worker is started in its own
--   background task. Workers emit commands that are dispatched to the
--   appropriate service handlers.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   -- Static (no config needed):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withService cartService
--     |&gt; Application.withInbound @() (_ -&gt; periodicCartCreator)
--   
--   -- Config-dependent:
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withConfig @MyConfig
--     |&gt; Application.withInbound (cfg -&gt; makeInbound cfg)
--   </pre>
withInbound :: Typeable config => (config -> Inbound) -> Application -> Application

-- | Register an ADR-0055 outbound integration type.
--   
--   At startup, <a>run</a> parses the <tt>--integrations=</tt> flag,
--   builds a pre-bound closure for each registered type, and stores them
--   in a <a>DispatchRegistry</a>. Domain code then calls <a>emit</a> (or
--   the global wrapper) to dispatch without knowing whether real or fake
--   ran.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withIntegration @SendEmail
--     |&gt; Application.withIntegration @ChargeIntent
--   </pre>
withIntegration :: (Integration request, Typeable request, Typeable (Response request), Inspectable request) => Application -> Application

-- | Enable JWT authentication for WebTransport.
--   
--   The factory takes a function from your config type to the auth server
--   URL. If you don't need app config, use <tt>()</tt> as the config type:
--   
--   <pre>
--   -- Static URL (no app config needed):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withAuth @() (\_ -&gt; "<a>https://auth.example.com")</a>
--   
--   -- Config-dependent (uses app config):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withConfig @AppConfig
--     |&gt; Application.withAuth (\cfg -&gt; cfg.authServerUrl)
--   </pre>
--   
--   When the config type is <tt>()</tt>, the factory is evaluated
--   immediately and no <a>withConfig</a> is required. Otherwise,
--   <a>withConfig</a> must be called first and the factory is evaluated
--   during <a>run</a>.
--   
--   For advanced configuration with overrides, use
--   <a>withAuthOverrides</a>.
withAuth :: Typeable config => (config -> Text) -> Application -> Application

-- | Enable JWT authentication with custom configuration.
--   
--   This version allows overriding auth settings like audience, clock
--   skew, etc. The factory takes a function from your config type to the
--   auth server URL.
--   
--   <pre>
--   let overrides = Auth.Config.defaultOverrides
--         { audience = Just "my-api"
--         , permissionsClaim = Just "scope"
--         }
--   
--   -- Static URL with overrides:
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withAuthOverrides @() (\_ -&gt; "<a>https://auth.example.com")</a> overrides
--   
--   -- Config-dependent URL with overrides:
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withConfig @AppConfig
--     |&gt; Application.withAuthOverrides (\cfg -&gt; cfg.authServerUrl) overrides
--   </pre>
withAuthOverrides :: Typeable config => (config -> Text) -> AuthOverrides -> Application -> Application

-- | Configure API metadata for OpenAPI spec generation.
--   
--   This sets the title, version, and description that appear in the
--   OpenAPI specification. If not called, sensible defaults are used.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withTransport WebTransport.server
--     |&gt; Application.withApiInfo "My API" "1.0.0" "A comprehensive API"
--   </pre>
withApiInfo :: Typeable config => (config -> ApiInfo) -> Application -> Application

-- | Configure a custom SecretStore for token storage.
--   
--   By default, OAuth2 uses an in-memory SecretStore which is suitable for
--   development. For production, provide a persistent implementation
--   (e.g., Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, encrypted database).
--   
--   <pre>
--   -- Development (default - in-memory):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2StateKey <a>OAUTH2_STATE_KEY</a>
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2Provider @() (\_ -&gt; ouraConfig)
--   
--   -- Production (custom implementation):
--   vaultStore &lt;- MyVault.newSecretStore vaultConfig
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withSecretStore vaultStore
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2StateKey <a>OAUTH2_STATE_KEY</a>
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2Provider @() (\_ -&gt; ouraConfig)
--   </pre>
withSecretStore :: Typeable config => (config -> SecretStore) -> Application -> Application

-- | Initialize OAuth2 with an HMAC key loaded from environment.
--   
--   The HMAC key is used to sign state tokens for CSRF protection. It is
--   loaded from the specified environment variable at runtime. The key
--   MUST be at least 32 bytes (256 bits) for security.
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2StateKey <a>OAUTH2_STATE_KEY</a>
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2Provider @() (\_ -&gt; ouraConfig)
--   </pre>
withOAuth2StateKey :: Text -> Application -> Application

-- | Add an OAuth2 provider to the Application.
--   
--   This enables OAuth2 provider integration routes:
--   
--   <ul>
--   <li><tt>GET <i>connect</i>{provider}</tt> - Initiate OAuth2 flow</li>
--   <li><tt>GET <i>callback</i>{provider}</tt> - OAuth2 callback</li>
--   <li><tt>POST <i>disconnect</i>{provider}</tt> - Disconnect
--   provider</li>
--   </ul>
--   
--   Requires <a>withOAuth2StateKey</a> to be called first. Requires JWT
--   authentication to be enabled (via withAuth).
--   
--   The factory takes a function from your config type to
--   OAuth2ProviderConfig. If you don't need app config, use <tt>()</tt> as
--   the config type:
--   
--   <pre>
--   -- Static config (no app config needed):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2StateKey <a>OAUTH2_STATE_KEY</a>
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2Provider @() (\_ -&gt; ouraConfig)
--   
--   -- Config-dependent (uses app config):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withConfig @AppConfig
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2StateKey <a>OAUTH2_STATE_KEY</a>
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2Provider (\cfg -&gt; makeOuraConfig cfg.ouraClientId cfg.ouraClientSecret)
--   </pre>
--   
--   Multiple providers can be added by calling this function multiple
--   times:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withTransport WebTransport.server
--     |&gt; Application.withAuth @() (\_ -&gt; "<a>https://auth.example.com")</a>
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2StateKey <a>OAUTH2_STATE_KEY</a>
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2Provider @() (\_ -&gt; ouraConfig)
--     |&gt; Application.withOAuth2Provider @() (\_ -&gt; githubConfig)
--   </pre>
withOAuth2Provider :: Typeable config => (config -> OAuth2ProviderConfig) -> Application -> Application

-- | Configure file uploads for the Application.
--   
--   This adds file upload and download routes:
--   
--   <ul>
--   <li><tt>POST <i>files</i>upload</tt> - Upload a file (multipart
--   form)</li>
--   <li><tt>GET <i>files</i>{fileRef}</tt> - Download a file</li>
--   </ul>
--   
--   The factory takes a function from your config type to
--   FileUploadConfig. If you don't need app config, use <tt>()</tt> as the
--   config type:
--   
--   <pre>
--   -- Static config (no app config needed):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withFileUpload @() (\_ -&gt; fileUploadConfig)
--   
--   -- Config-dependent (uses app config):
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withConfig @AppConfig
--     |&gt; Application.withFileUpload (\cfg -&gt; makeFileUploadConfig cfg)
--   </pre>
--   
--   When the config type is <tt>()</tt>, the factory is evaluated
--   immediately and no <a>withConfig</a> is required. Otherwise,
--   <a>withConfig</a> must be called first and the factory is evaluated
--   during <a>run</a>.
withFileUpload :: Typeable config => (config -> FileUploadConfig) -> Application -> Application

-- | Configure CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) for WebTransport.
--   
--   When configured, all HTTP responses include CORS headers and OPTIONS
--   preflight requests are handled automatically.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withTransport WebTransport.server
--     |&gt; Application.withCors Web.CorsConfig
--         { allowedOrigins = ["<a>http://localhost:5173"]</a>
--         , allowedMethods = [<a>GET</a>, <a>POST</a>, <a>OPTIONS</a>]
--         , allowedHeaders = ["Content-Type", <a>Authorization</a>]
--         , maxAge = Just 3600
--         }
--   </pre>
withCors :: Typeable config => (config -> CorsConfig) -> Application -> Application

-- | Customize the health check endpoint path.
--   
--   By default, WebTransport serves a health endpoint at <tt>GET
--   /health</tt> that returns <tt>200 OK</tt> with
--   <tt>{"status":"ok"}</tt>. Use this to change the path.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withTransport WebTransport.server
--     |&gt; Application.withHealthCheck "_health"
--     |&gt; Application.withService myService
--   </pre>
withHealthCheck :: Typeable config => (config -> Text) -> Application -> Application

-- | Disable the automatic health check endpoint.
--   
--   By default, WebTransport serves a health endpoint at <tt>GET
--   /health</tt>. Use this to disable it entirely (e.g., if you need
--   custom health logic).
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withTransport WebTransport.server
--     |&gt; Application.withoutHealthCheck
--     |&gt; Application.withService myService
--   </pre>
withoutHealthCheck :: Application -> Application

-- | Configure the integration dispatcher.
--   
--   The dispatcher routes events to per-entity workers for outbound
--   integrations. By default, <a>defaultConfig</a> is used which sets a
--   30-second event processing timeout.
--   
--   Use this to customize timeouts, channel capacity, and worker lifecycle
--   for your application's needs.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withDispatcherConfig
--          Dispatcher.defaultConfig { eventProcessingTimeoutMs = Just 120000 }
--     |&gt; Application.withService myService
--   </pre>
withDispatcherConfig :: Typeable config => (config -> DispatcherConfig) -> Application -> Application

-- | Wire a persistent query object store backend into the application.
--   
--   Equivalent to <a>withQueryObjectStore</a>; provided for
--   discoverability in the builder chain alongside <a>withQuery</a> and
--   <a>withService</a>.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withEventStore postgresEventStoreConfig
--     |&gt; Application.useQueryObjectStore (PostgresQueryObjectStoreConfig { ... })
--     |&gt; Application.withQuery @UserOrders
--     |&gt; Application.run
--   </pre>
useQueryObjectStore :: QueryObjectStoreConfig config => config -> Application -> Application

-- | Enable the /ready HTTP endpoint (on by default, shown for
--   discoverability).
--   
--   The /ready endpoint returns 200 when all registered queries have
--   completed their rebuild, and 503 while any query is still replaying.
--   Calling this function is optional — the endpoint is on by default. It
--   is exposed in the public API so Jess can see the feature and
--   understand the <i>health vs </i>ready distinction without reading
--   prose.
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   app = Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.useReadinessEndpoint
--     |&gt; Application.withQuery @UserOrders
--     |&gt; Application.run
--   </pre>
useReadinessEndpoint :: Application -> Application

-- | Health check configuration for WebTransport. When enabled, GET
--   requests to the health path return 200 OK with a JSON body. Enabled by
--   default with path "health". Can be disabled or customized.
data HealthCheckConfig
HealthCheckConfig :: Text -> HealthCheckConfig

-- | URL path segment for the health endpoint (default: "health"). The
--   endpoint will be available at /{healthPath}.
[healthPath] :: HealthCheckConfig -> Text

-- | Configuration for the dispatcher.
data DispatcherConfig
DispatcherConfig :: Int -> Int -> Bool -> Int -> Int -> Maybe Int -> Int -> Int -> DispatcherConfig

-- | How long a worker can be idle before being reaped (milliseconds).
--   Default: 60000 (60 seconds)
[idleTimeoutMs] :: DispatcherConfig -> Int

-- | How often the reaper checks for idle workers (milliseconds). Default:
--   10000 (10 seconds)
[reaperIntervalMs] :: DispatcherConfig -> Int

-- | Whether to enable the reaper. Set to False for testing. Default: True
[enableReaper] :: DispatcherConfig -> Bool

-- | Capacity of worker channels (bounded). Events queue up to this limit
--   before backpressure is applied. Default: 100 Sizing rule of thumb:
--   capacity = processing_rate * acceptable_latency
[workerChannelCapacity] :: DispatcherConfig -> Int

-- | Timeout in milliseconds for writing to a full channel. If exceeded,
--   event is logged as dropped. Default: 5000 (5 seconds)
[channelWriteTimeoutMs] :: DispatcherConfig -> Int

-- | Timeout in milliseconds for processing a single event. If exceeded,
--   the event processing is cancelled and the worker continues with the
--   next event. Default: Nothing (no timeout). This prevents slow
--   integrations from blocking a worker indefinitely.
[eventProcessingTimeoutMs] :: DispatcherConfig -> Maybe Int

-- | Maximum number of retry attempts for transient IO failures in outbound
--   event processing. Only IO exceptions (e.g., network errors) are
--   retried; application-level errors (e.g., JSON decode failures) are
--   not. Default: 3 (total attempts = initial + 3 retries)
[maxEventRetries] :: DispatcherConfig -> Int

-- | Base delay in milliseconds for exponential backoff between retries.
--   Actual delays: baseDelay * 2^attempt (e.g., 500ms, 1000ms, 2000ms).
--   Default: 500
[retryBaseDelayMs] :: DispatcherConfig -> Int

-- | Default dispatcher configuration.
defaultConfig :: DispatcherConfig

-- | Internal configuration for file upload routes (after initialization).
--   
--   This is created by <tt>initializeFileUpload</tt> from a
--   <tt>FileUploadConfig</tt>. Users should not create this directly - use
--   <tt>FileUploadConfig</tt> instead.
data FileUploadSetup
FileUploadSetup :: InternalFileUploadConfig -> BlobStore -> FileStateStore -> FileUploadSetup

-- | Upload limits and allowed content types
[config] :: FileUploadSetup -> InternalFileUploadConfig

-- | Where to store file content
[blobStore] :: FileUploadSetup -> BlobStore

-- | Where to track file lifecycle state
[stateStore] :: FileUploadSetup -> FileStateStore

-- | Check if the Application is empty (no configurations set).
isEmpty :: Application -> Bool

-- | Check if a config has been registered.
hasConfig :: Application -> Bool

-- | Check if a QueryRegistry has been configured.
hasQueryRegistry :: Application -> Bool

-- | Check if any ServiceRunners have been configured.
hasServiceRunners :: Application -> Bool

-- | Get the number of ServiceRunners configured.
serviceRunnerCount :: Application -> Int

-- | Check if any transports have been configured.
hasTransports :: Application -> Bool

-- | Get the number of transports configured.
transportCount :: Application -> Int

-- | Check if any query endpoints have been configured.
hasQueryEndpoints :: Application -> Bool

-- | Get the number of query endpoints configured.
queryEndpointCount :: Application -> Int

-- | Check if any query definitions have been registered.
hasQueryDefinitions :: Application -> Bool

-- | Get the number of query definitions registered.
queryDefinitionCount :: Application -> Int

-- | Check if an EventStore has been configured.
hasEventStore :: Application -> Bool

-- | Check if file upload has been configured.
hasFileUpload :: Application -> Bool

-- | Check if authentication has been configured.
hasAuth :: Application -> Bool

-- | Run the application using the configured EventStore.
--   
--   This is the primary way to run an Application. It: 1. Creates the
--   EventStore from the configured settings 2. Wires all query definitions
--   3. Rebuilds queries from historical events 4. Starts live subscription
--   for new events 5. Runs all services
--   
--   Example:
--   
--   <pre>
--   Application.new
--     |&gt; Application.withEventStore postgresConfig
--     |&gt; Application.withTransport WebTransport.server
--     |&gt; Application.withService myService
--     |&gt; Application.withQuery @CartSummary
--     |&gt; Application.run
--   </pre>
run :: Application -> Task Text Unit

-- | Run application with a provided EventStore.
--   
--   Use this when you need to provide your own EventStore instance. For
--   most cases, prefer using <a>run</a> with <a>withEventStore</a>.
--   
--   NOTE: This function only supports file uploads configured with
--   <tt>withFileUpload @()</tt> (i.e., config type is <tt>()</tt>). If
--   your FileUpload factory uses app config, use <a>run</a> instead, which
--   loads config before resolving factories.
--   
--   This function: 1. Wires all query definitions (creates stores,
--   updaters, endpoints) 2. Creates a query subscriber with combined
--   registries 3. Rebuilds all queries from historical events 4. Starts
--   live subscription for new events 5. Collects all command endpoints
--   from all services 6. Runs each transport once with combined endpoints
runWith :: EventStore Value -> Application -> Task Text Unit

-- | Run application with provided EventStore, non-blocking.
--   
--   Launches the application in a background task and returns immediately.
--   Errors are logged to the console rather than silently discarded.
runWithAsync :: EventStore Value -> Application -> Task Text Unit

module Auth.OAuth2.TokenRefresh

-- | Errors that can occur during token refresh operations.
data TokenRefreshError err

-- | No tokens stored for userId. The Text parameter is the userId (NOT the
--   full key). Example: TokenNotFound "user-123" (not
--   "oauth:provider:user-123")
TokenNotFound :: Text -> TokenRefreshError err

-- | Refresh token is missing from stored token set
RefreshTokenMissing :: TokenRefreshError err

-- | Token refresh request failed
RefreshFailed :: OAuth2Error -> TokenRefreshError err

-- | Storage operation failed
StorageError :: Text -> TokenRefreshError err

-- | Custom action failed (e.g., HTTP request)
ActionFailed :: err -> TokenRefreshError err

-- | Per-key lock acquisition timed out (prevents deadlock). The Text
--   parameter is the lock key (format: "providerName:userId"). SECURITY:
--   Lock key does NOT contain userId alone — it is safe to log.
LockAcquisitionTimeout :: Text -> TokenRefreshError err

-- | Execute an action with automatic token refresh on unauthorized errors.
--   
--   This function: 1. Retrieves tokens from SecretStore using key
--   "oauth:{providerName}:{userId}" 2. Executes the action with the access
--   token 3. If the predicate identifies an "unauthorized" error,
--   refreshes the token 4. Uses per-key locking to prevent duplicate
--   concurrent refreshes (#333) 5. Stores the new TokenSet and retries the
--   action ONCE
--   
--   The refresh action is injectable for testability. In production, pass:
--   <tt>Auth.OAuth2.Client.refreshTokenValidated validatedProvider
--   clientId clientSecret</tt>
--   
--   Example production usage:
--   
--   <pre>
--   withValidToken store refreshLocks "oura" userId
--     (OAuth2.refreshTokenValidated validatedProvider clientId clientSecret)
--     isUnauthorized
--     myAction
--   </pre>
--   
--   Example test usage with mock:
--   
--   <pre>
--   withValidToken mockStore mockLocks "provider" "user1"
--     mockRefreshSuccess
--     isUnauthorized
--     (\token -&gt; Task.yield [fmt|got #{token}|])
--   </pre>
withValidToken :: SecretStore -> ConcurrentMap Text Lock -> Text -> Text -> (RefreshToken -> Task OAuth2Error TokenSet) -> (err -> Bool) -> (Text -> Task err value) -> Task (TokenRefreshError err) value
instance GHC.Classes.Eq err => GHC.Classes.Eq (Auth.OAuth2.TokenRefresh.TokenRefreshError err)
instance GHC.Generics.Generic (Auth.OAuth2.TokenRefresh.TokenRefreshError err)
instance GHC.Show.Show err => GHC.Show.Show (Auth.OAuth2.TokenRefresh.TokenRefreshError err)
